meta.purpose: Classify each radical constraint as invariant (structurally necessary), typical (usually present but not required), or contingent (depends on tactical variation, transition stage, or camera realization)
meta.method: Analysis of constraint satisfaction rates, BJJ positional mechanics, and adversarial edge cases

constraints.justification: Mount requires at least one leg straddling the opponent's torso. Without this, the position is not mount by any standard definition. The right-leg contact is the more frequently detected of the two bilateral contacts.
constraints.counterexample: S-mount (one leg posted forward, one behind head) — leg is still ON torso axis, just repositioned. The contact exists but the axis alignment changes. No known mount variant lacks leg-on-torso contact entirely.

constraints.justification: BOTH legs straddling the torso is the textbook mount definition. However, several common mount variants have only one leg tightly on the torso: technical mount (one leg posted behind), S-mount, gift-wrap mount. In these, the second leg contact is absent or repositioned. 56% satisfaction suggests this contact is present in only ~60% of what the dataset calls mount.
constraints.counterexample: Technical mount: one knee on mat beside opponent's hip, other leg posted behind head. Only one leg contacts the torso in the standard axis sense.

constraints.justification: The mount player always faces the mounted player. The low satisfaction rate (46%) is entirely a 2D inference problem — the shoulder-normal dot product is unreliable under projection. In 3D, facing-opposed is definitional for mount vs back control. No mount variant has fighters facing the same direction.
constraints.note: The 46% rate does NOT mean facing-opposed is contingent. It means the measurement is bad. A blind audit would likely confirm facing-opposed at 95%+.

constraints.justification: The mounted player is on the ground by definition. 98.3% satisfaction confirms this. The 1.7% failure is measurement noise (hip-height comparison).

MNT.summary: 3 invariant, 1 typical. The second leg contact (Me.Le-) should arguably be demoted from required to typical, or the radical should be split into 'full mount' (both legs) and 'technical mount' (one leg).

constraints.justification: Back control requires hooks (legs around torso). At least one hook is definitional.
Both hooks is standard but not invariant. Body triangle uses one hook + leg lock. Single-hook back control is common in transitions. 73% satisfaction reflects the variety of hook configurations.
constraints.counterexample: Body triangle back control: one leg threads behind the opponent, locking at the ankle with the other. The contact graph is different from bilateral hooks.

constraints.justification: Back control means being behind the opponent. Both face the same direction by definition. 81% satisfaction is reasonable given 2D measurement noise.

The controlled person is frequently flat on the mat (prone). Back control can occur with the opponent seated (back-take from guard), prone (flattened out), or on their side. The elevation state is NOT structurally necessary — it is a tactical detail that varies by entry and opponent response. 70% satisfaction confirms it is common but not invariant.
constraints.counterexample: Back mount (opponent flat on stomach, controller on top with hooks): opponent is ON the ground, not elevated.

constraints.justification: Ankle closure (feet locked) distinguishes closed guard from back control. If the controller's ankles are locked AND facing aligned, the position would be a body triangle variant, which is structurally distinct. However, body triangle IS a form of back control, so this forbidden contact may be too strict. 91% satisfaction means 9% of back control samples have detected ankle closure — likely false positives from the proximity-based detection.

BCTR.summary: 2 invariant, 1 typical, 1 contingent, 1 invariant-but-borderline. NotOnGround(Op.Ba) is contingent and should be removed. The second hook contact is typical.

constraints.justification: Side control is defined by upper-body pressure — at minimum one arm controlling the opponent's torso. This is the most fundamental side control contact. 87% satisfaction on raw data supports invariance.
constraints.note: Sign-specific (Ar+, right arm) may be too strict. Side control can be performed from either side. An unsigned variant (Ar, either arm) would be more robust.

constraints.justification: The opponent in side control is on the ground. Definitional. 91% satisfaction, 9% failure is ground-detection noise.

Intended to exclude guard positions (where legs are entangled). However, several side control variants involve leg positioning near the opponent's legs: kesa gatame (scarf hold) has legs splayed near opponent, reverse kesa has leg contact. The prohibition is based on proximity detection which cannot distinguish entanglement from adjacency. 22% violation rate on true SCTR data confirms the constraint fires on legitimate side control.
constraints.counterexample: Kesa gatame: top player's legs are on the mat, one leg near opponent's hip/leg. Geometrically close but not entangled.

constraints.justification: Same issue as the other forbidden leg contact. Symmetric pair; both are contingent.

SCTR.summary: 2 invariant, 2 contingent. The forbidden leg contacts should be removed or converted to a bilateral forbidden (both legs entangled simultaneously = guard, not just one leg nearby).

constraints.justification: Closed guard requires legs around the opponent's torso. At least one leg must be there. 89% on raw data supports invariance.

The second leg around the torso. Unlike mount (where one leg can be posted), closed guard genuinely requires BOTH legs — the guard player is supine and both legs wrap the opponent. The 66% satisfaction is a perception issue (left-leg occlusion), not a structural one. Closed guard without both legs around the torso is open guard by definition.
constraints.note: The lower rate vs the first leg is systematic left-side occlusion, not a notation problem.

constraints.justification: Ankle closure is THE defining feature of closed guard vs open guard. Without locked ankles, the position is open guard. This is definitional, not contingent. The 46% satisfaction rate is purely a 2D detection failure.
constraints.note: However, the DATASET may tag some open-guard-with-legs-around-torso positions as 'closed_guard'. If so, the low rate reflects tag imprecision (A), not notation error (D). A blind audit would resolve this.

constraints.justification: The opponent in closed guard is in the guard player's legs, not flat on the ground. 99.9% satisfaction confirms this.

CGRD.summary: 4 invariant. All CGRD constraints are structurally necessary. Low satisfaction rates are perception issues. However, this assessment ASSUMES the dataset tags are correct for 'closed guard' — if the dataset includes open-guard positions tagged as closed guard, the ankle closure constraint would appear to fail.

constraints.justification: Half guard is defined by one leg entangling the opponent's leg. Without this, it is not half guard. The unsigned sign (Le, either leg) is appropriate — half guard can use either leg.

Standard half guard has fighters facing each other. However, several variants do not: lockdown half guard (bottom player faces away), deep half guard (bottom player perpendicular or under the opponent), dogfight/whip-up (bottom player turning away). 62% satisfaction reflects genuine variation, not just measurement error.
constraints.counterexample: Deep half guard: bottom player is underneath the top player, facing the ceiling or perpendicular. Chest-to-chest facing is not present.

constraints.justification: The half guard player is on the ground by definition (they are the bottom player). 98% satisfaction confirms.

HGRD.summary: 2 invariant, 1 typical. FacingOpposed is typical for half guard, not invariant. Deep half guard and lockdown are legitimate half guard variants without facing-opposed.

constraints.justification: Turtle position involves one player draped over the other's back. Torso-on-torso contact is definitional. 0% satisfaction is entirely a 2D detection failure. The contact EXISTS in the position; the inference pipeline cannot measure it.
constraints.note: HOWEVER: this assessment is unfalsifiable from the current data. We assume the contact is present because it should be. A blind audit is needed to determine if annotators confirm torso contact in TRTL-tagged images.

constraints.justification: In turtle, both fighters face the same direction (top behind bottom). This is definitional. The 50% satisfaction rate is a 2D measurement failure.
If the top player's legs are around the bottom player's torso, the position is back control, not turtle. This forbidden contact correctly distinguishes TRTL from BCTR.
Symmetric with the other forbidden contact.

TRTL.summary: All 4 invariant. However, the required contact and facing frame are UNTESTABLE from 2D data, so the invariant classification for the contact is an assumption.

constraints.justification: Most open guard positions have fighters facing each other, but not all. Single-leg X, crab ride, and some inverted guard positions break this. Additionally, open guard transitions often pass through non-facing-opposed states. The 46% satisfaction rate is partly measurement error and partly genuine variation.
constraints.counterexample: Crab ride: guard player faces away from opponent while controlling their legs.

constraints.justification: The open guard player is on the ground (bottom player). Definitional. 99% satisfaction.

OGRD.summary: 1 invariant, 1 typical. OGRD is problematic as a radical because it has only frame constraints and no contacts, making it a frame envelope rather than a positional specification. See ogrd_ontology_analysis.md.

constraints_requiring_change: BCTR: NotOnGround(Op.Ba) — contingent, should be removed
SCTR: CON(Me.Le->Op.Le+ h=-) forbidden — contingent, should be removed or converted
SCTR: CON(Me.Le->Op.Le- h=-) forbidden — contingent, should be removed or converted
MNT: CON(Me.Le-->Op.To h=+) — typical, should be reconsidered or split
HGRD: FacingOpposed() — typical, consider demotion to soft constraint
